question.text: An option contract differs from a futures contract because:

answer.text: An option gives the buyer the right but not the obligation, while futures create an obligation for both parties
Options are always OTC while futures are always exchange-traded
Options have no expiry date while futures expire on fixed dates
Options can only be used for hedging while futures can be used for speculation

explanation.text: **The Logic:** The fundamental difference is obligation vs. right. In futures, both buyer and seller are obligated to settle. In options, the buyer has the right (not obligation) to buy/sell, while the seller (writer) has the obligation if the buyer exercises. **The Trap:** Options trade both OTC AND on exchanges. Both options and futures have expiry dates. **Cross-Exam Context:** Options vs futures distinction is fundamental to Series I, VIII, and IV. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The option buyer pays a premium for the right. The option seller receives the premium but takes on the obligation.
